### Workshop Purpose

This report summarizes the Ghana R Conference 2026 workshop project: **AI-Driven Predictive Analytics for Business Growth Using R: Insights from African Market Use Cases**. The project uses Nigerian housing listings as a practical real-estate market-intelligence use case.

The business question is straightforward: **can we use housing market data to estimate property value and support smarter real estate growth decisions?** The workflow demonstrates how raw African market data can move through validation, feature engineering, model training, model comparison, stakeholder reporting, and deployment-aware handoff artifacts.

### Overall Conclusion

The project remains a decision-support workflow, not an automated valuation authority. Its strongest value is that it gives investors, developers, property technology teams, lenders, and REIT-style analysts a repeatable analytical process for pricing guidance, market screening, and portfolio review.

When the current artifact set is available, the selected champion model is shown in @tbl-best-model. The model comparison stage evaluates a simple baseline against stronger tree-based methods so participants can see the business value of disciplined model selection rather than relying on a single algorithm.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-best-model
#| tbl-cap: Best model performance summary

make_best_model_tbl(report_artifacts$best_model_metrics)
```

### Key Workshop Takeaways

1. **Business framing comes before modeling.** The prediction target is useful only when connected to pricing, investment screening, and market-expansion decisions.
2. **Validation is part of the product.** Data checks, run manifests, and report artifacts make the workflow easier to trust and repeat.
3. **Model performance must be interpreted carefully.** The model is trained on `log_price`, while stakeholder-facing predictions are transformed back into Naira for interpretation.
4. **Price gaps are screening signals, not final investment decisions.** A listing below the model estimate should trigger due diligence, not automatic purchase action.

{{< pagebreak >}}

### Reproducible Workflow

The report follows the same reproducible pattern as the reference project: data and modeling logic live in `R/`, narrative sections live in `qmd/`, and the root `report.qmd` assembles the final stakeholder deliverable.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-reproducible-workflow
%%| fig-cap: Reproducible workshop reporting workflow
%%| fig-align: center

flowchart LR
A[(Raw Nigeria housing CSV)] --> B[Data validation]
B --> C[Feature engineering]
C --> D[Model training and tuning]
D --> E[Evaluation artifacts]
E --> F[[Quarto stakeholder report]]
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Why this matters for the workshop

Participants are not only learning how to fit a model in R. They are learning how to build a traceable analytics workflow where every chart, metric, recommendation, and report table can be regenerated from the project folder.
:::
